did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the YOLOv11 deep learning object detection framework for multi-class skin lesion detection using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. It evaluates architectural variations, including replacing the backbone with MobileNetV3 and the detection head with an SSD-style head, to analyze detection accuracy and computational efficiency for skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a baseline YOLOv11 model and the evaluation of architectural variations (MobileNetV3 backbone, SSD head) on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology, dataset preparation, training parameters (epochs, batch size, optimizer), and performance evaluation metrics (mAP, precision, recall, F1-score). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICITIIT), it reports original results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Architectural Variations of YOLO for Skin Cancer Detection: Backbone–Head Flip Analysis
journal: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Trends in Information Technology (ICITIIT)
brief_summary: This study investigates the application of the YOLOv11 real-time object detection framework for multi-class skin lesion detection using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The research evaluates five clinically significant lesion categories (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, MEL, and NV) by establishing a baseline YOLOv11 model and exploring architectural variations, specifically replacing the backbone with MobileNetV3 and the detection head with an SSD-style head. Performance was assessed using standard metrics including mAP, precision, recall, F1-score, and inference time. Experimental results demonstrated that the baseline YOLOv11 configuration achieved the highest overall accuracy with an mAP@0.5 of 96.8%, outperforming earlier variants like YOLOv8s and YOLOv9-n. While lightweight variants offered insights into computational efficiency trade-offs suitable for edge deployment, they exhibited a slight decrease in detection accuracy compared to the baseline. The findings highlight the adaptability of modern object detection frameworks for medical image analysis and provide a foundation for efficient AI-assisted skin cancer detection systems.

paper_terms: Pre-cancerous lesion due to long-term sun exposure
lesion_types_studied.details: One of five selected lesion categories from the HAM10000 dataset. Described as pre-cancerous. Low-frequency class with 94-95% recall due to class imbalance effects.
One of five selected lesion categories from the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a common skin cancer from basal cells with medium risk.
One of five selected lesion categories from the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a non-cancerous pigmented or keratinized growth with low risk. Exceeded 99% precision and recall.
One of five selected lesion categories from the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a malignant tumor from melanocytes with high risk. Visually similar to benign nevi.
One of five selected lesion categories from the HAM10000 dataset. Described as a common benign mole with low risk. Exceeded 99% precision and recall.

all_task_types.target: Bounding boxes and class labels for five skin lesion categories (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, MEL, NV) in dermoscopic images.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes the task as 'multi-class skin lesion detection' using the YOLOv11 object detection framework. The model predicts bounding boxes for localization and class labels for classification simultaneously. Performance is evaluated using object detection metrics such as mAP@0.5, precision, recall, and F1-score. While classification is a component of the detection head, the primary task is detection/localization of lesions within images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset containing skin lesion images. The study utilized a subset of this dataset containing five selected skin lesion categories.

use.training: Used for training the baseline YOLOv11 model and architectural variations (MobileNetV3 backbone, SSD head) after preprocessing and augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation to evaluate detection performance, compute metrics (mAP, precision, recall, F1-score), and generate confusion matrices.
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate final detection performance and inference time.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the baseline YOLOv11 model and architectural variations (MobileNetV3 backbone, SSD head) were based on standard object detection metrics (mAP, precision, recall, F1-score) and inference time without statistical significance testing.

ssd_head.metric_description: Competitive inference performance with reduced computational overhead; marginally lower localization precision than default YOLOv11 head

conclusions: The baseline YOLOv11 model demonstrated strong multi-class skin lesion detection performance on the HAM10000 dataset, achieving an mAP@0.5 of 96.8%, outperforming previous YOLO variants such as YOLOv5s, YOLOv8s, and YOLOv9-n.

Replacing the default YOLOv11 backbone with MobileNetV3 reduced computational complexity and improved inference speed, but resulted in a slight decrease in detection accuracy, highlighting the trade-off between model compactness and feature representation strength.

Replacing the YOLOv11 detection head with an SSD-style head provided competitive inference performance with reduced computational overhead, though the default YOLOv11 head maintained a better balance between localization precision and classification performance.

Common lesion types (Benign Keratosis-like Lesion and Melanocytic Nevus) exceeded 99% precision and recall, while low-frequency classes (Actinic Keratosis) averaged 94–95% recall, indicating that class imbalance remains a challenge for minority categories despite augmentation strategies.
Mosaic augmentation and Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) were effective in mitigating the under-detection of small lesions.

The study confirms the adaptability of modern object detection frameworks like YOLOv11 for medical image analysis and suggests potential suitability for future edge deployment scenarios, provided the minor trade-off in detection accuracy is acceptable.
Future work should explore class-weighted loss functions or synthetic data generation to improve the detection performance of rare lesion categories.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance and demographic bias (predominantly fair-skinned populations), limiting the generalizability of the results to diverse skin types or external clinical settings.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific distribution.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between the baseline YOLOv11 and the architectural variations, relying instead on point estimates of metrics like mAP and precision.

Class imbalance remains a critical limitation; rare lesion categories (e.g., AKIEC) showed lower recall (94-95%) compared to common classes (BKL, NV >99%), indicating potential clinical risk for missed diagnoses of less frequent but potentially serious conditions.

The paper acknowledges limited model interpretability as a barrier to clinical adoption, noting that medical practitioners require transparent and explainable decision-making systems, which were not addressed in this work.

While lightweight variants (MobileNetV3 backbone, SSD head) improved inference speed, they resulted in a slight decrease in detection accuracy, highlighting a trade-off that must be carefully considered for real-world deployment.
The research is published in a conference proceeding (ICITIIT) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; the paper mentions splitting data into training, validation, and test sets but does not detail how patient-level separation was ensured to prevent image-level leakage.
